rationale: The page provides detailed factual information on Asian Elephants, including their characteristics, habitat, and conservation status. It also features a comparative table between Asian and African elephants and a section on the 50th anniversary of India's Crocodile Conservation Project. This content is highly suitable for generating multiple-choice or descriptive questions.
cleanedText:
Asian Elephants (Elephas maximus)
Characteristics
- Largest land mammal on Asian continent.
- 3 Sub-species: Indian, Sumatran and Sri Lankan.
- Highly intelligent animals with strong family bonds and sophisticated forms of communication.
- Complex social structures led by a matriarch (a female leads the group).
- Life span: 60-70 years.
- Longest gestation period in mammals: 22 months.
Habitat
- Dry to wet forest and scrubland/grassland habitats in 13 range countries.
- Role of Elephants in Ecosystem: Create pathways for other animals in dense forest, creation of micro ecosystems, and seed dispersal maintaining forest diversity.
Conservation Measures
- Project Elephant: Launched in 1992.
- 33 Elephant Reserves in 14 major Elephant states.
Legal and Conservation Status
- WPA, 1972: Schedule I
- CITES: Appendix I
- IUCN Status: EN (Endangered)
Difference between Asian and African Elephants
- Parameters: Species
- Asian Elephants: N/A
- African Elephants: Classified into 2 separate species: African Savanna Elephant (Endangered) and African Forest Elephant (Critically Endangered)
- Parameters: Size
- Asian Elephants: Smaller
- African Elephants: Larger
- Parameters: Head shape
- Asian Elephants: Twin-domed with a central indent
- African Elephants: Rounded/Flat single dome
- Parameters: Ears
- Asian Elephants: Small, rounded
- African Elephants: Savannah Elephants: Large, fan-shaped; Forest Elephants: Perfectly oval/round
- Parameters: Tusks
- Asian Elephants: Only some males have visible tusks, female may have smaller tusks.
- African Elephants: Both male and female have larger tusks. Savannah elephants: Curved, thick, outward-pointing; Forest elephants: Straight, thin, downward-pointing
- Parameters: Back
- Asian Elephants: Humped/convex, curved outward
- African Elephants: Concave/ dipped
1.1.11. 50 YEARS OF CROCODILE CONSERVATION PROJECT
Why in the News?
- On the eve of World Crocodile Day (June 17) India celebrated 50 years of its Crocodile Conservation Project.
About Crocodile Conservation Project
- Project Launch: April, 1975 based on H.R. Bustard’s recommendations.
- Objective: Protect Crocodile’s natural habitats and rebuild the population quickly through captive breeding.
Crocodile Conservation Success in India
- Odisha: Only state with conservation centres for all 3 native crocodilian species.
